name: Selkie
primary_sources: Oral traditions of the Northern Isles of Scotland

visual_attributes.morphology: Shapeshifting creature with both seal and human forms
distinctive_features: Ability to remove or don seal skin to transform
Attractive and seductive appearance in human form
visual_attributes.texture: Smooth and sleek in seal form, human-like skin in human form
visual_attributes.size_and_scale: Seal form similar to large seals like the Greenland or hooded seal
visual_attributes.structural_arrangement: Seal skin can be removed to reveal human form underneath

domain_specific_visual.Cultural_Iconography: Often depicted near the sea or ocean
domain_specific_visual.Transformation_Motif: Visual emphasis on the dual nature of seal and human

functional_attributes.primary_function: Shapeshifting between seal and human forms
secondary_functions: Romantic or sexual relationships with humans

domain_specific_functional.Narrative_Role: Symbolizes the tension between freedom and captivity
domain_specific_functional.Cultural_Motif: Represents the allure and danger of the sea

relationships.description: Selkies are sometimes confused with mermaids due to their seal-like attributes
Selkies are sometimes conflated with finfolk in Shetland traditions

contextual_attributes.geographic_range: Northern Isles of Scotland, Orkney, Shetland
contextual_attributes.cultural_significance: Represents the connection and conflict between land and sea
